**Joakim Agustín Soria Ramos** (born May 18, 1984) is a Mexican former professional [baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball") [pitcher](/wiki/Pitcher "Pitcher"). He played in [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") (MLB) for the [Kansas City Royals](/wiki/Kansas_City_Royals "Kansas City Royals"), [Texas Rangers](/wiki/Texas_Rangers_(baseball) "Texas Rangers (baseball)"), [Detroit Tigers](/wiki/Detroit_Tigers "Detroit Tigers"), [Pittsburgh Pirates](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Pirates "Pittsburgh Pirates"), [Chicago White Sox](/wiki/Chicago_White_Sox "Chicago White Sox"), [Milwaukee Brewers](/wiki/Milwaukee_Brewers "Milwaukee Brewers"), [Oakland Athletics](/wiki/Oakland_Athletics "Oakland Athletics"), [Arizona Diamondbacks](/wiki/Arizona_Diamondbacks "Arizona Diamondbacks"), and [Toronto Blue Jays](/wiki/Toronto_Blue_Jays "Toronto Blue Jays") from 2007 to 2021.

Soria began his professional career in the [Mexican League](/wiki/Mexican_Baseball_League "Mexican Baseball League"), before briefly playing in [minor league baseball](/wiki/Minor_league_baseball "Minor league baseball") for the [Los Angeles Dodgers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Dodgers "Los Angeles Dodgers") and [San Diego Padres](/wiki/San_Diego_Padres "San Diego Padres") organizations. The Royals selected Soria from the Padres in the [Rule 5 draft](/wiki/Rule_5_draft "Rule 5 draft") after the 2006 season, and he debuted with the Royals in 2007. Soria is a two-time [MLB All-Star](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_All-Star_Game "Major League Baseball All-Star Game").

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joakim_Soria&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Career")]

### Early career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joakim_Soria&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early career")]

Soria has played for his country's [Diablos Rojos del México](/wiki/Diablos_Rojos_del_M%C3%A9xico "Diablos Rojos del México") (in the [Mexican Baseball League](/wiki/Mexican_Baseball_League "Mexican Baseball League")) and [Yaquis de Obregón](/wiki/Yaquis_de_Obreg%C3%B3n "Yaquis de Obregón") in the [Winter League](/wiki/Mexican_Pacific_League "Mexican Pacific League"). He played for the Class-A [Fort Wayne Wizards](/wiki/Fort_Wayne_Wizards "Fort Wayne Wizards") as well. On December 9, 2006, in the Mexican Winter League, Soria threw a [perfect game](/wiki/Perfect_game_(baseball) "Perfect game (baseball)") against the [Naranjeros de Hermosillo](/wiki/Naranjeros_de_Hermosillo "Naranjeros de Hermosillo") at the [Estadio Tomás Oroz Gaytán](/wiki/Estadio_Tom%C3%A1s_Oroz_Gayt%C3%A1n "Estadio Tomás Oroz Gaytán").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

### Kansas City Royals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joakim_Soria&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Kansas City Royals")]

In 2006, Soria was drafted at the age of 22 by the [Kansas City Royals](/wiki/Kansas_City_Royals "Kansas City Royals") in the [Rule 5](/wiki/Rule_5 "Rule 5") draft out of the [San Diego Padres](/wiki/San_Diego_Padres "San Diego Padres") organization in 2006.[[3]](#cite_note-Soria_heading-3) He debuted for the Royals in the 2007 season, and had a [win–loss record](/wiki/Win%E2%80%93loss_record_(pitching) "Win–loss record (pitching)") of 2–3 with 17 [saves](/wiki/Save_(baseball) "Save (baseball)") and an [earned run average](/wiki/Earned_run_average "Earned run average") (ERA) of 2.48.

Soria opened the 2008 season with 13 straight saves, breaking [Al Hrabosky](/wiki/Al_Hrabosky "Al Hrabosky")'s club record of 11.[[3]](#cite_note-Soria_heading-3) He was scored on in just two of 35 outings.[[3]](#cite_note-Soria_heading-3) He went 161⁄3 innings before giving up a run and in one stretch retired 24 straight batters.[[3]](#cite_note-Soria_heading-3)

On May 17, 2008, Soria signed a three-year, $8.75 million extension to his contract with the Royals, following his impressive performance as the team's closer in the first quarter of the season.[[4]](#cite_note-MLB-4)

Soria's fantastic start led to him being selected to play in the [2008 Major League Baseball All-Star Game](/wiki/2008_Major_League_Baseball_All-Star_Game "2008 Major League Baseball All-Star Game").[[3]](#cite_note-Soria_heading-3) Soria was the Royals' lone representative in the All-Star Game.[[3]](#cite_note-Soria_heading-3) He was the first Royals closing pitcher to be named an All-Star since [Mike MacDougal](/wiki/Mike_MacDougal "Mike MacDougal") in [2003](/wiki/2003_Major_League_Baseball_All-Star_Game "2003 Major League Baseball All-Star Game").[[3]](#cite_note-Soria_heading-3) He pitched 1+2⁄3 innings, striking out the [Florida Marlins](/wiki/Florida_Marlins "Florida Marlins")' [Dan Uggla](/wiki/Dan_Uggla "Dan Uggla") and [New York Mets](/wiki/New_York_Mets "New York Mets")' [David Wright](/wiki/David_Wright "David Wright").[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Joakim_Soria_on_July_10%2C_2013.jpg/250px-Joakim_Soria_on_July_10%2C_2013.jpg)](/wiki/File:Joakim_Soria_on_July_10,_2013.jpg)

Soria pitching for the Texas Rangers

Soria had injuries but he finished the 2009 year strong with a 3–2 record with 30 saves out of 33 chances.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

On May 26, 2010, Soria got his 100th save against the [Texas Rangers](/wiki/Texas_Rangers_(baseball) "Texas Rangers (baseball)").[[8]](#cite_note-8)
Soria was picked for the [2010 All-Star Game](/wiki/2010_Major_League_Baseball_All-Star_Game "2010 Major League Baseball All-Star Game"), the second of his career. He finished the season with a 1.78 ERA and 43 saves in 46 opportunities.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Soria struggled out of the gate in the 2011 season, blowing five of his first twelve save opportunities. After blowing saves on back to back days, Soria was replaced as closer by [Aaron Crow](/wiki/Aaron_Crow "Aaron Crow") on May 30.[[10]](#cite_note-10) After a couple of good relief appearances in a non-closer role, Yost announced on June 6 that Soria had earned the position back.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

On April 3, 2012, Soria underwent [Tommy John surgery](/wiki/Tommy_John_surgery "Tommy John surgery") to repair a damaged [ulnar collateral ligament](/wiki/Ulnar_collateral_ligament_of_the_elbow "Ulnar collateral ligament of the elbow") in his right elbow, causing him to miss the entire 2012 season.[[12]](#cite_note-12) The Royals declined his 2013 option on October 31, making him a free agent.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Texas Rangers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joakim_Soria&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Texas Rangers")]

On December 4, 2012, Soria signed a two-year contract worth $8 million with the [Texas Rangers](/wiki/Texas_Rangers_(baseball) "Texas Rangers (baseball)") with a club option for the 2015 season.[[14]](#cite_note-14) He started the season on the 60-day disabled list as he was still recovering from [surgery](/wiki/Tommy_John_surgery "Tommy John surgery"). He made his first appearance for Texas on July 7, 2013.[[15]](#cite_note-15) After former closer [Joe Nathan](/wiki/Joe_Nathan "Joe Nathan") signed with the [Detroit Tigers](/wiki/Detroit_Tigers "Detroit Tigers") in the offseason, Soria was named the new Rangers closer for the 2014 season.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

### Detroit Tigers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joakim_Soria&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Detroit Tigers")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Joakim_Soria_with_Detroit_Tigers.jpg/250px-Joakim_Soria_with_Detroit_Tigers.jpg)](/wiki/File:Joakim_Soria_with_Detroit_Tigers.jpg)

Soria pitching for the Detroit Tigers

On July 23, 2014, the Texas Rangers traded Soria to the [Detroit Tigers](/wiki/Detroit_Tigers "Detroit Tigers") in exchange for pitchers [Jake Thompson](/wiki/Jake_Thompson "Jake Thompson") and [Corey Knebel](/wiki/Corey_Knebel "Corey Knebel").[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18) Soria recorded his 200th career save on July 22, 2015.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Pittsburgh Pirates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joakim_Soria&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Pittsburgh Pirates")]

On July 30, 2015, the Tigers traded Soria to the [Pittsburgh Pirates](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Pirates "Pittsburgh Pirates") in exchange for [JaCoby Jones](/wiki/JaCoby_Jones "JaCoby Jones").[[20]](#cite_note-20)

### Second stint with the Royals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joakim_Soria&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Second stint with the Royals")]

On December 10, 2015, Soria signed a three-year, $25 million contract to return to the Royals.
[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### Chicago White Sox

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joakim_Soria&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Chicago White Sox")]

On January 4, 2018, Soria was traded to the [Chicago White Sox](/wiki/Chicago_White_Sox "Chicago White Sox") in a three team trade that also sent Jake Peter and [Scott Alexander](/wiki/Scott_Alexander_(baseball) "Scott Alexander (baseball)") to the [Los Angeles Dodgers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Dodgers "Los Angeles Dodgers"), [Luis Avilán](/wiki/Luis_Avil%C3%A1n "Luis Avilán") to the White Sox and [Trevor Oaks](/wiki/Trevor_Oaks "Trevor Oaks") and [Erick Mejia](/wiki/Erick_Mejia "Erick Mejia") to the Royals.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### Milwaukee Brewers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joakim_Soria&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Milwaukee Brewers")]

On July 26, 2018, Soria was traded to the [Milwaukee Brewers](/wiki/Milwaukee_Brewers "Milwaukee Brewers") in exchange for [Kodi Medeiros](/wiki/Kodi_Medeiros "Kodi Medeiros") and Wilber Pérez.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Soria declined his half of a mutual option for the 2019 season and became a free agent, on October 30, 2018.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### Oakland Athletics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joakim_Soria&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Oakland Athletics")]

On December 21, 2018, Soria signed a two-year contract with the [Oakland Athletics](/wiki/Oakland_Athletics "Oakland Athletics").[[25]](#cite_note-25) On June 25, 2019, Soria pitched in his 674th major league game, passing [Dennys Reyes](/wiki/Dennys_Reyes "Dennys Reyes") for the most by a Mexican-born pitcher.[[26]](#cite_note-26) In 2019 for the Athletics, Soria pitched to a 4.30 ERA over 69 innings pitched in 71 appearances, to go along with 79 strikeouts. In 2020 for Oakland, Soria made 22 appearances, pitching 22+1⁄3 innings with a 2.82 ERA and 24 strikeouts.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

### Arizona Diamondbacks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joakim_Soria&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Arizona Diamondbacks")]

On February 6, 2021, Soria officially signed with the [Arizona Diamondbacks](/wiki/Arizona_Diamondbacks "Arizona Diamondbacks") on a one-year, $3.5 million contract.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

### Toronto Blue Jays

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joakim_Soria&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Toronto Blue Jays")]

On July 30, 2021, Soria was traded to the [Toronto Blue Jays](/wiki/Toronto_Blue_Jays "Toronto Blue Jays") in exchange for minor leaguers J.J. D'Orazio and Yaifer Perdomo.[[29]](#cite_note-29) After the 2021 season, Soria announced his retirement.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

## Pitch selection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joakim_Soria&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Pitch selection")]

Soria throws five pitches. He relies primarily on his [fastball](/wiki/Fastball "Fastball") and [cut fastball](/wiki/Cut_fastball "Cut fastball"). Both pitches average about 90 miles per hour (140 km/h), with the fastball topping out near 94 miles per hour (151 km/h) and the cutter topping out around 93 miles per hour (150 km/h). He mixes in a slider in the low 80s, a slow [curveball](/wiki/Curveball "Curveball") around 70 miles per hour (110 km/h), and an occasional [changeup](/wiki/Changeup "Changeup") in the low- to mid-80s.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joakim_Soria&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Soria and his wife, Karla, have three children.[[32]](#cite_note-32) Soria is a [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity").[[33]](#cite_note-SS-33)

Soria is a supporter of the charity [Water Mission](/wiki/Water_Mission "Water Mission").[[33]](#cite_note-SS-33)

Soria has been nicknamed *The Mexicutioner*,[[3]](#cite_note-Soria_heading-3) although in February 2011, he stated that he no longer wanted to be associated with that nickname due to violence in his home country.[[34]](#cite_note-34)
